·" 
CommissioIL 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assiotance 
(916) 322·5662 
James R. Christiansen 
City Attorney 
P.O. Box 278 
Carpinteria, CA 
93013 
Dear Mr. Christiansen: 
... Administration ••• 
Executive/legaJ 
••• 
322.5660 
322·5901 
January 10, 1979 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Opinion Request 
No. 78-015 
Conflict of Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter of December 13, 1978, requesting 
an opinion regarding the conflict of interest provisions 
under the Political Reform Act. 
Since your letter raises no 
substantial question of interpretation under the Act, no 
formal opinion will be issued. 
However, I hope the following 
informal advice provided pursuant to Government Code Section 
83114(b) answers your questions. 
The facts as I understand them are as follows. 
The 
Architectural Advisory Board ("Board") participates in the 
official recommendation process to the Planning Commission 
and the City Council regarding development projects in the 
City of Carpinteria. 
The members of the Board are unpaid. 
However, several members are practicing architects in Carpinteria 
and surrounding areas and as such have projects of their own 
which may occasionally come before the Planning Commission 
and City Council. 
Some of these projects may also have 
received advisory approvals by the Board. 
The Board members 
do not participate in the consideration of any projects in 
which they have been involved as the architect or in any 
other capacity. 
. 
As you know, Government Code Section 1090, et ~., is 
not part of the Political Reform Act ("Act") and, therefore, 
I cannot comment on its interpretation. 
With respect to the 
Act, you asked four questions. 
First, you asked whether a project which was designed 
by a Board member may be reviewed by the Board. 
Nothing in 


James R. 
Chri~tiansen 
,J anuary 10, 1979 
¢page Two 
.) 
the Act would prohibit the Board from considering the project. 
However, if the Board is a decision-making body within the 
meaning of the Fair Political Practices Commission's regulation, 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8700(a){1), the member who designed 
the project would be required to disqualify himself from 
making, participating in the making or using his official 
position to influence the Board's decision or recommendation. 
Second, you asked whether a member of the Board who 
designed a project which was approved by the Board could 
become an advocate for the project before the Planning Com-
mission or City Council. 
A member of the Board, in his 
private capacity, may become an advocate before the Planning 
Commission and City Council since he is neither a member or 
employee of nor does he have budgetary control over either 
such body. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8700(e}. 
Third, you asked whether a Board member may participate 
in the consideration of a project designed by another archi-
tect on behalf of a developer who is a source of income to 
the Board member. 
The answer to this question is the same 
as the answers to the two previous questions. 
If the Board 
is a decision-making body pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
l8700{a)(1), the Board member may not participate in decisions 
which foreseeably will have a material financial effect on 
the developer. 
In his private capacity, however, the Board 
member may appear before the Planning Commission and City 
Council on behalf of the developer without violating the 
Act. 
Fourth, you asked whether the answers to questions two 
and three would be different if the project did not require 
approval by the Board. 
The answer is no. 
If you wish to appeal the denial of your request for a 
formal opinion, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18321. 
If you have any questions regarding this 
matter, please contact Ted Prim, Chief of the Conflicts of 
Interest Division. 
MB:TP:plh 
Enclosures: 
Sincerely, 
TZJl~~\. i IJ), Jf6:~~ ~4-" 
Michael Bennett 
Executive Director 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 
18700 and 18702

sections.question: First, whether a project which was designed by a Board member may be reviewed by the Board. Second, whether a member of the Board who designed a project which was approved by the Board could become an advocate for the project before the Planning Commission or City Council. Third, whether a Board member may participate in the consideration of a project designed by another architect on behalf of a developer who is a source of income to the Board member. Fourth, whether the answers to questions two and three would be different if the project did not require approval by the Board.
sections.conclusion: First, nothing in the Act would prohibit the Board from considering a project designed by a Board member, but if the Board is a decision-making body, the member must disqualify himself. Second, a Board member may become an advocate in his private capacity before the Planning Commission and City Council since he is neither a member, employee of, nor has budgetary control over such bodies. Third, if the Board is a decision-making body, a Board member may not participate in decisions that will foreseeably have material financial effect on a developer who is a source of income to the member, but may appear before other bodies in his private capacity. Fourth, the answers to questions two and three would not be different if the project did not require Board approval.
sections.question_synthetic: Can members of an unpaid Architectural Advisory Board, who are practicing architects with their own projects, participate in Board decisions and recommendations regarding projects in which they have a financial interest or professional involvement, and can they subsequently advocate for those projects before other bodies?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Board members who are practicing architects may generally not participate in Board decisions regarding projects where they have a financial interest or professional involvement, but may advocate for such projects in their private capacity before other bodies like the Planning Commission and City Council.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter issued under Government Code Section 83114(b) rather than formal opinion; contains four distinct questions with corresponding answers; discusses application of FPPC regulations 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700 and 18702.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: First, whether a project which was designed by a Board member may be reviewed by the Board. Second, whether a member of the Board who designed a project which was approved by the Board could become an advocate for the project before the Planning Commission or City Council. Third, whether a Board member may participate in the consideration of a project designed by another architect on behalf of a developer who is a source of income to the Board member. Fourth, whether the answers to questions two and three would be different if the project did not require approval by the Board.

CONCLUSION: First, nothing in the Act would prohibit the Board from considering a project designed by a Board member, but if the Board is a decision-making body, the member must disqualify himself. Second, a Board member may become an advocate in his private capacity before the Planning Commission and City Council since he is neither a member, employee of, nor has budgetary control over such bodies. Third, if the Board is a decision-making body, a Board member may not participate in decisions that will foreseeably have material financial effect on a developer who is a source of income to the member, but may appear before other bodies in his private capacity. Fourth, the answers to questions two and three would not be different if the project did not require Board approval.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provided informal advice to the City Attorney of Carpinteria regarding conflict of interest issues affecting members of an Architectural Advisory Board who are also practicing architects with potential financial interests in projects reviewed by the Board.
